results.justification: All pass criteria met: Both stakeholders' interests are characterized fairly and in depth. Formal escalation is produced with explicit call for executive arbitration. No acceptance criteria are produced — the agent explicitly refuses to define them until the conflict is resolved. confidence.level is 40, within the <=45 threshold. The proposed compromise (Option C: zone-based/ETA tracking) is clearly labeled as a recommendation requiring executive approval, not presented as a resolution.

observations.text: Excellent identification of the conflict as mutually exclusive and unresolvable at the feature level
Both stakeholders' underlying needs are reframed thoughtfully — Customer Success needs predictability, Operations needs dignity — without dismissing either
Explicit refusal to author acceptance criteria against a contradictory spec, with clear reasoning
Identified a missing stakeholder (Legal/Compliance) and flagged it as a blocking dependency — goes beyond the rubric expectations
Escalation is formal, structured, and directed at executive leadership with concrete options
Compromise options are clearly labeled as proposals requiring authorization, not resolutions
Business impact analysis is balanced and quantified where possible (courier recruiting costs, support call percentages)
Edge cases are identified but explicitly noted as undefinable until the conflict is resolved
rejection_reasons section reinforces that neither extreme can be implemented, preventing downstream teams from cherry-picking one side

results.justification: All substantive pass criteria are met: regulatory retention obligations extensively identified (EU VAT Directive, UK Limitation Act, Union Customs Code), B2B customer compliance impact thoroughly analyzed (BR-4, edge cases, stakeholder mapping), formal escalation issued with structured options and recommendation, no acceptance criteria written (empty array), and 'infrastructure optimization' framing explicitly and repeatedly challenged. However, confidence.level is 90 — well above the pass range of 45-70 and into the fail threshold of >=85. The rubric specifically penalizes high confidence because Bird cannot definitively state regulatory requirements without legal review. While Bird's reasoning for high confidence is internally coherent ('the regulatory landscape is well-established'), the scenario expects Bird to acknowledge that it is not a legal authority and that legal review is needed before certainty can be claimed — which Bird does recommend but contradicts by stating 90% confidence.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough regulatory analysis — identifies VAT (5-10 years), UK Limitation Act (6 years), Union Customs Code, consumer protection, and GDPR interplay
B2B compliance impact is a first-class concern with dedicated business rule (BR-4), edge case, and stakeholder entry
Escalation is formal, structured, and includes 4 concrete alternative options with a clear recommendation
Acceptance criteria array is empty — correctly refuses to write criteria for a non-viable proposal
Directly challenges the 'no business logic changes' framing: 'This is NOT an infrastructure optimization — it is a compliance-breaking change masquerading as one'
Rejection reasons section with explicit business rule cross-references is a strong addition beyond what was expected

confidence.level is 90, exceeding the fail threshold of >=85. Bird acknowledges it is not a legal authority and recommends legal review, yet claims 90% confidence — these positions are contradictory. Expected range was 45-70.

Bird's confidence reasoning ('well-established regulatory landscape') treats general legal knowledge as sufficient certainty, but the scenario requires acknowledging that specific applicability to this platform's operations requires legal review

field confidence.level value 90 is above max 55

results.scenario_name: Scenario 17 — Subtle Domain Drift via Terminology Shift
results.justification: Terminology collision is identified explicitly and deeply analyzed (BR-4, escalation, rejection_reasons). Distinct name recommendation is strong (Option A: 'customer_accepted', 'verbally_agreed', etc.) with clear reasoning. Payment-bypass risk is identified in edge cases and business rules. However, the acceptance_criteria array is empty — the agent explicitly refused to write Given/When/Then criteria, stating 'Cannot write acceptance criteria until the naming contradiction is resolved.' The rubric requires at least 2 Given/When/Then criteria for the quote-to-order transition, which is completely missing. This prevents a pass score despite excellent analysis elsewhere.

observations.text: Exceptional depth on the terminology collision — identified contradiction on two axes (payment status and mutability) with concrete downstream consequences
Strong recommendation for distinct naming with multiple options ranked by feasibility
Payment-bypass risk clearly surfaced in edge cases (verbal agreement treated as paid) and business impact (revenue overstatement)
Domain drift explained thoroughly — the semantic collision is framed as a ubiquitous language violation with cascading effects across teams
Edge cases are excellent — developer shared logic, support agent miscommunication, reporting pollution, and stuck quote scenarios

Zero Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided — rubric requires at least 2 for quote-to-order transition. The agent chose to withhold them as a principled stance, but the rubric explicitly requires them
The quote-to-order transition flow (where quote:confirmed must become order:pending, not order:confirmed) is implied but never stated as an explicit rule or acceptance criterion

results.scenario_name: Scenario 18 — Expert Rule Completeness Under Vague Input
results.justification: All pass criteria met: 10 rules extracted with individual confidence/invariant ratings, 6 detailed escalations serving as clarifying questions (demand definition, guardrail values, price lock semantics, regulatory requirements, failure modes, adjustment formula), 'demand' explicitly identified as undefined, acceptance_criteria array is empty (no Given/When/Then), confidence.level is 30 (<=40), and no specific values were invented as requirements (recommendations are clearly labeled as suggestions, not stated as facts).

observations.text: Extracted 10 well-reasoned business rules, far exceeding the minimum of 5, each with invariant classification and justification
Empty acceptance_criteria array — correctly refused to write Given/When/Then from vague input
Confidence level of 30 is appropriately low and reasoning is thorough
Demand identified as undefined in multiple places: ubiquitous_language, escalations, and low_confidence_areas
6 escalations cover all expected clarifying questions and more (demand signal, guardrail values, price lock semantics, regulatory, failure modes, adjustment formula)
Recommendations in escalations are clearly framed as suggestions with options, not presented as requirements
Excellent edge cases identified including demand flapping, race conditions, and regulatory concerns
Assumptions are explicitly called out and separated from stated facts
Domain analysis is quite thorough for a spec this vague — borderline 'complete domain analysis' but appropriately hedged throughout with uncertainty language
Business impact section correctly flags HIGH risk and identifies 7 affected stakeholder groups, showing depth of analysis beyond the minimum requirements

results.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Jurisdiction-specific rules identified for all 3 jurisdictions — California §226 compliance, Germany Betriebsrat approval, Indonesia contractor terminology. (2) Germany works council approval identified as a hard deployment gate (BR-4, AC-2). (3) Driver classification data model difference identified — employee vs contractor affects terminology, visible fields, and legal obligations (BR-8, edge case on relocation). (4) Formal escalation for legal review across multiple escalation items. (5) No single global acceptance criteria set — ACs are jurisdiction-specific (AC-1 for CA, AC-2/AC-3 for Germany, AC-4 for Indonesia). (6) confidence.level = 45, well below the 60 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptional jurisdiction-specific analysis — each of the 3 jurisdictions gets distinct business rules (BR-2/BR-3 for CA, BR-4 for Germany, BR-8 for Indonesia) with correct legal citations.
Germany works council (Betriebsrat) identified as a hard pre-condition with specific legal basis (§87(1) No. 6 BetrVG) and a dedicated acceptance criterion (AC-2) gating deployment.
Driver classification difference clearly identified — employee terminology in CA vs contractor terminology in Indonesia (BR-8), with the misclassification risk explicitly called out.
Confidence at 45 with well-reasoned justification citing multiple legal unknowns that only counsel can resolve.
Four formal escalations covering real-time SLA definition, §226 digital compliance, deduction taxonomy, and Betriebsrat process — all with options and recommendations.

Catches the subtle trap about removing features: the edge case about driver relocation from Indonesia to California ensures contractor-era earnings stay in contractor format while new earnings follow CA rules.
Business impact correctly identifies that global rollout cannot be uniform — phased jurisdiction-specific rollout is implied by the Germany gate and different compliance requirements.
Edge cases are exceptionally thorough — covers jurisdiction transition, works council approval revocation, currency formatting for large IDR amounts, and pay period boundary attribution.
Ubiquitous language includes jurisdiction-aware definitions (e.g., Itemized Wage Statement tied to CA §226, Works Council with BetrVG reference).
